narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 47% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes, focusing on who tends to pass the bar right away. The model's decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the single strongest positive influence, as it signaled to the model a higher likelihood of passing, countering some risks. In contrast, your LSAT score of 27—noticeably below the average of about 37—pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor, reflecting how lower scores in large datasets often correlate with bar struggles. Your third-year high school decile ranking of 3, lower than the typical 5.5, also nudged the outlook negative, suggesting the model saw early academic inconsistencies as a hurdle. While your middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with most applicants, they didn't sway the balance much here. Compared to peers who got approved, your LSAT and later decile placed you below the stronger performers who typically pass the bar at higher rates. Your stronger undergrad record shows real promise, though, setting you apart from many whose GPAs lag behind. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—plenty succeed despite similar starts—but the model prioritizes these bar predictors to minimize risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, as it could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening recent academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework might also help. Reach out to admissions for feedback, and explore bridge programs or retake strategies that have worked for others in your position—your GPA gives you a great foundation to build on.
